Similarity(\vec{X}, \vec{Y}) = \cos(\theta) = \frac{\tilde{X}\cdot\tilde{Y}}{\|\tilde{X}\|\,\|\tilde{Y}\|} = \frac{\sum_{i} x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum_{i} x_i^2}\,\sqrt{\sum_{i} y_i^2}}
Word analogies reveal structured relationships in the embedding space and expose model biases.
Machine learning models learn the biases present in the training data; the distance between embeddings reflects these biases.
Language Models are neural‑network‑based algorithms designed to understand and generate human language text, learning probability distributions over sequences of words.
Applications include text understanding (question answering, sentiment analysis) and text generation (sentence completion, captioning, translation, story generation, etc.).
Challenges of working with text versus images include handling grammar, spelling, a large vocabulary, and decisions about tokenization (words vs subwords, etc.).
Sentiment analysis is the task of identifying the sentiment a text conveys.
Typical application domains include movie reviews, product feedback, emails, and tweets.
The problem involves arbitrary‑length input and output and can be applied to different languages.
Sentiment140 consists of 1,600,000 tweets collected by students, where sentiment is inferred from emoticons: :) indicates positive sentiment and :( indicates negative sentiment.

For each tweet: (1) split the tweet into words; (2) look up the GloVe embedding for each word, ignoring words without embeddings; (3) add up the word embeddings to obtain a single embedding for the entire tweet; (4) feed the tweet embedding into a fully‑connected neural network.
GloVe embeddings are pre‑trained dense vector representations of words.
Using a simple summation of word embeddings discards word order, causing sentences with different meanings to receive identical embeddings.
The sentences "The food was adequate, but just not great" and "The food was not just adequate, but great" produce the same embedding under the summation model, despite opposite meanings.
Concatenate the word embeddings of a tweet and feed the concatenated vector to a fully‑connected neural network, preserving word order.
The input size to the fully‑connected network varies with tweet length, making the architecture inconsistent.
Applying padding to achieve a fixed input size would dramatically increase the number of parameters, leading to inefficiency.
Concatenate the word embeddings and process the resulting sequence with a 1‑dimensional convolutional neural network (1D CNN), which handles variable‑length inputs more efficiently.
While addressing the sizing problem, this approach introduces a new issue related to (text truncated in source).

Convolution is local: this means that the model will miss the long‑range dependency that comes with sentences. Elements (words) that are far apart in a string may still be highly dependent on each other for context.
Can take in variable‑sized sequential input.
Can remember things over time, i.e., it has some sort of memory or state.
RNN uses its past to determine its future.
Many real‑world problems involve inputs/outputs with varying sizes and require data from the past to accurately model future data.
RNN layers have a recursive process that has not been seen in any other type of network so far.
The hidden state is updated until we run out of tokens.
Updating Hidden State: hidden state is updated based on previous hidden state and the input using the same neural network as before (weight sharing).
The last hidden state is used as input to a prediction network (fully‑connected network).

Context Vector: a fixed‑size representation that summarizes the information from the entire input sequence processed by the RNN. It captures the context or relevant information from past time steps and serves as an input to subsequent parts of the network, allowing the RNN to maintain memory and make predictions based on the entire sequence history.
If we consider the concatenated input/hidden and output/hidden vectors as simply input/output, the forward path in an RNN is equivalent to a fully‑connected neural network.
Predictions or decisions made based on the entire sequence as a whole.
Common in tasks such as text summarization, machine summarization, and sequence‑to‑sequence tasks like language translation.
The model generates an output sequence that summarizes or transforms the input sequence, and the quality of the whole generated sequence is evaluated rather than individual tokens.
Predictions or labels for individual elements within a sequence, such as words in a sentence or characters in a text.
Useful for tasks like part‑of‑speech tagging, named entity recognition, sentiment analysis, and machine translation, where each element is annotated or classified independently.
RNNs can become very deep; depth equals the length of the sequence.
Vanilla RNNs are not good at modelling long‑term dependencies.
Training is difficult due to vanishing and exploding gradients.
Exploding gradients occur when gradient magnitudes become excessively large, while vanishing gradients occur when they shrink toward zero, both hindering effective learning.
If a gradient exceeds a predefined threshold, it is set (clipped) to that threshold value.
Gradient clipping is used to mitigate exploding gradients in deep or long‑unrolled RNNs.
Skip connections can be introduced to alleviate vanishing gradients by providing direct pathways to earlier hidden states.
Ideally, skip connections would link to all previous states, but this is computationally expensive.
Preserving the hidden state or context over the long term can be achieved through selective skip connections.

Gating Mechanism: a set of learnable components that regulate the flow of information through the network’s hidden states. Gating mechanisms, such as those used in Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) cells, allow the network to selectively update, forget, or pass along information from previous time steps, enabling better modeling of long‑term dependencies and mitigating issues like vanishing gradients.
Gating mechanism controls how much information flows through the network.
Approximate skip‑connections to all previous states by learning to weight previous states differently (soft skip‑connections).
Use gates that learn to update the context selectively.
f(x) = X \cdot \sigma(X)   (where \sigma is the sigmoid or tanh activation)
f(x) = X \cdot NN(X)   (where NN denotes a learned neural‑network function)

Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM): a type of recurrent neural network (RNN) architecture designed to model sequential data by effectively capturing and preserving long‑term dependencies. LSTMs use a specialized cell with gating mechanisms to control the flow of information, allowing them to store, update, and retrieve information over extended sequences.
LSTMs consist of a long‑term memory (cell state) and a short‑term memory (context or hidden state).
Forget Gate (long‑term memory): determines how much of the past memory should be forgotten.
Input Gate (long‑term memory): determines how much the current input should contribute to the memory.

Gated Recurrent Unit: a type of recurrent neural network (RNN) architecture that is designed for modeling sequential data. It simplifies the architecture of Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks by using fewer gates but still effectively captures and manages information over time. GRUs have gate mechanisms that control the flow of information, helping them mitigate the vanishing gradient problem and enabling the modeling of long-term dependencies in sequential data, making them suitable for various tasks such as natural language processing and time series analysis.
GRUs are more efficient than LSTMs while having similar performance.
They combine forget and input gates into an update gate.
They also merge cell state and hidden state.
LSTMs/GRUs can be trained on longer sequences and are much better at learning long-term relationships.
They are easier to train and achieve better performance than vanilla RNNs.
LSTM/GRU haven’t finished converging here; with more training they do better.

Bidirectional RNN: a type of neural network architecture that processes input sequences in both forward and reverse directions simultaneously. This allows them to capture information from past and future time steps, making them particularly useful for tasks.
Context from both directions is essential, such as natural language understanding and speech recognition.
A typical state in an RNN (RNN, GRU, or LSTM) relies on the past and the present.
In tasks such as machine translation, where a prediction depends on the past, present, and future, we can exploit the future to improve performance.

Deep RNN: a type of recurrent neural network architecture that consists of multiple recurrent layers stacked on top of each other. These additional layers enable the network to capture more complex hierarchical features and representations from sequential data, making them suitable for tasks that require a deep understanding of temporal dependencies and context.
Stacking RNN layers allows learning more abstract representations.
Representations in first layers are better for syntactic tasks (low level) while representations in last layers perform better on semantic tasks (high level).
Various RNN configurations in PyTorch, including many-to-one and many-to-many (same length) setups, as well as specialized setups for translation, image captioning, etc.
Sequence-to-Sequence Models: models where both input and output are sequences, often implemented with an encoder‑decoder architecture.
Sequence-to-Sequence models are generative and can be thought of like autoencoders.
In the encoder, tokens are processed one at a time; the hidden state represents all the tokens read thus far.
In the decoder, tokens are generated one at a time; the hidden state is a representation of all the tokens to be generated.
The hidden state serves as a summary of past inputs for the encoder and a summary of generated outputs for the decoder.
Generating variable‑length sequences requires a mechanism to know when to stop; dedicated control symbols <BOS> (Beginning of Sequence) and <EOS> (End of Sequence) are used.
<BOS> denotes the beginning of a sequence; <EOS> denotes the end of a sequence.
Training‑time behavior must be changed using teacher forcing; added randomness (e.g., in variational autoencoders) may also be introduced.
Teacher forcing forces the RNN to stay close to the ground‑truth sequence by feeding the ground‑truth token as the next input instead of the model's own prediction.
During training, loss is computed at each step by comparing the RNN's prediction with the ground‑truth token using cross‑entropy.
Inference‑time behavior changes by using sampling and temperature scaling to produce diverse, non‑deterministic outputs.
Greedy Search selects the token with the highest probability as the generated token.
maxp(t1, t2, ... tn) = maxp(t1) \cdot p(t2) \cdot ... \cdot p(tn)
Three sampling strategies are considered: greedy search, beam search, and other stochastic methods (e.g., temperature‑scaled sampling).
Beam search is mentioned as an alternative sampling strategy that keeps multiple candidate sequences during generation.
Temperature scaling adjusts the softness of the probability distribution during sampling, controlling the randomness of generated tokens.
Beam Search: looks for a sequence of tokens with the highest probability within a window.
Softmax Temperature Scaling: helps with the problem of over-confidence in neural networks by scaling the input logits to the softmax with a temperature.
Low Temperature (larger logits, more confident): Higher quality samples, less variety.
High Temperature (smaller logits, less confident): Lower quality samples, more variety.

Generative Model: a type of model that learns to model the probability distribution of the input data. It can generate new data points that resemble the training data, making it capable of creating synthetic data. Examples include Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs).

Discriminative Model: a model that learns to model the decision boundary between different classes or categories in the data. It’s primarily used for classification tasks, aiming to distinguish between different classes based on input features. Examples include Logistic Regression, Support Vector Machines (SVMs), and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for image classification.
One of the primary goals of GANs is to generate data that is indistinguishable from real data, useful for generating realistic images, videos, text, etc.
GANs have been used to create lifelike images of nonexistent people, animals, or scenes.
Language models and sequence-to-sequence models are self-supervised.
Discriminative models are supervised and learn to approximate the conditional probability p(y|x), i.e., the probability of a label y given input x.
Generative models are unsupervised and learn to approximate the marginal probability p(x), i.e., the probability distribution of the data itself.

Generative learning is an unsupervised learning task where a loss function serves as an auxiliary task with known answers, there is no ground‑truth for the ultimate generation task, and the goal is to learn the structure and distribution of the data rather than label assignments.
A loss function in generative learning acts as an auxiliary task that we know the answer to.
There is no ground truth with respect to the actual generation task we ultimately want to accomplish.
The objective is to learn the data's underlying structure and distribution rather than to assign labels.
A generative model is a model that can generate new data samples from some input encoding.
Unconditional generative models receive only random noise as input and therefore provide no direct control over the category or attributes of the generated output.

Conditional generative models receive additional conditioning information (e.g., a one‑hot target category vector, an embedding from another model such as a CNN, or other high‑level control signals) together with random noise, allowing the user to steer what the model generates.
One‑hot encoding of the target category can be concatenated with random noise to condition generation.
An embedding generated by another model (e.g., a CNN) can serve as conditioning information.
Major families include Autoregressive Models, Variational Autoencoders (VAEs), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), Flow‑Based Generative Models, and Diffusion Models.
Autoregressive Models generate data sequentially, modeling the joint distribution as a product of conditionals.
Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) learn a latent probabilistic representation and maximize a variational lower bound on the data likelihood.
Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) consist of a generator and a discriminator trained in a minimax game.
Flow‑Based Generative Models transform a simple base distribution into the data distribution via a series of invertible mappings.
Diffusion Models generate data by iteratively denoising a sample starting from pure noise.
Vanilla autoencoders trained with Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss tend to produce blurry images because the loss encourages the network to predict the average pixel value for ambiguous regions.
Mean Squared Error (MSE) loss computes the average of the squared differences between predicted and true pixel intensities.
The blur arises from the loss function’s tendency to minimize pixel‑wise error, which penalizes sharp but uncertain predictions.
GANs train two models simultaneously: a generator that tries to produce realistic data samples and a discriminator that tries to distinguish generated samples from real data.
The training objective is a minimax game where the generator minimizes the discriminator’s ability to correctly classify generated data, while the discriminator maximizes its classification accuracy.
The generator receives random noise (and optionally conditioning information) as input and outputs synthetic data.
Discriminator model: try to distinguish between real and fake images.
Generator network – Input → a noise vector – Output → a generated image.
Discriminator network – Input → an image – Output → a binary label (real vs fake).
Loss Function for MinMax Game Idea.
The discriminator learns weights to maximize the probability that it labels a real image as real and a generated image as fake.
Since there are two possibilities as output for the discriminator (true & false), the optimal loss function is Binary Cross-Entropy.
The generator learns weights to maximize the probability that the discriminator labels a generated (fake) image as real.
The loss function of the generator is the discriminator itself – if the generator wins, the discriminator loses and vice‑versa; there are only two possible scenarios.
Alternate between training the discriminator and training the generator.
GANs are notoriously difficult to train.
A training curve is no longer as helpful as in supervised learning; generator and discriminator losses tend to bounce up and down because both networks change over time.
Tuning hyperparameters is much more difficult because the training curve cannot reliably guide the process.
Fool the discriminator by generating real‑looking images.
In a GAN, the discriminator tries to maximize correct labeling of real vs. fake images, while the generator tries to make the discriminator label generated images as real.
When the discriminator becomes too good, small changes in the generator’s weights do not affect the discriminator’s output, providing no useful gradient signal for the generator to improve.

The discriminator is used as the loss function for the generator; if the discriminator is overly accurate, the generator receives no proper feedback (no gradients) and cannot be incrementally improved.
A failure mode where the generator produces a limited set of outputs (often a single output) instead of the full diversity of the data distribution.

If the generator repeatedly outputs the same sample, the optimal discriminator strategy is to reject that sample; however, if the discriminator gets trapped in a local optimum, it cannot adapt, allowing the generator to fool it by generating only one type of data (e.g., only digit ‘1’).

Recent GAN variants mitigate mode collapse by feeding the discriminator a small batch (set) of real or fake samples at once, enabling the discriminator to use the variety within the set as a feature for determining authenticity.
Training vanilla GANs is difficult because the MinMax optimization objective creates unstable dynamics, making it hard to observe numerical convergence during training.

The adversarial training objective of GANs, expressed as a minimax game: the generator minimizes the probability of the discriminator correctly identifying fake samples while the discriminator maximizes its ability to distinguish real from fake.
In GAN training, the discriminator’s output is directly used to compute the loss for the generator, turning the discriminator into a dynamic loss function that guides generator updates.

A point in the parameter space where the discriminator’s loss cannot be further reduced locally, even though a better global solution may exist; can cause the discriminator to become stuck and enable mode collapse.

Adversarial Attack: a deliberate manipulation of input data, typically to machine learning models, with the goal of causing the model to make incorrect predictions or classifications.
Goal: choose a small perturbation \(\epsilon\) on an image \(x\) so that a neural network \(f\) misclassifies \(x + \epsilon\).
f(x + \epsilon) =

Non-targeted attack – Minimize the probability that f(x + ϵ) = correct class. The attacker’s goal is to cause any form of misclassification or disrupt the model’s predictions without specifying a particular target class. The objective is to degrade the model’s performance without a specific end result in mind. Example: manipulate image of a cat so that it’s classified as anything other than a cat. Often easier to carry out because they do not require the attacker to have knowledge of the target class; they may use simpler techniques and do not necessarily need to find the smallest perturbation.

Targeted attack – Maximize the probability that f(x + ϵ) = target class. The attacker aims to cause a particular misclassification or a specific response from the machine‑learning model. The attacker has a clear target class or outcome in mind. Example: manipulate image of a cat so that it’s classified as a dog. Requires a deep understanding of the model’s decision boundaries and often involves optimization techniques to find the smallest possible perturbations that achieve the desired target outcome.
f(x + ϵ) = correct class (objective to minimize its probability for non‑targeted attacks).
f(x + ϵ) = target class (objective to maximize its probability for targeted attacks).
White‑box attacks – Assume the model is known; the attacker has access to the architecture and weights of f and can directly optimize the perturbation ϵ.

Black‑box attacks – The attacker does not know the architecture or weights of f. Instead, a substitute (proxy) model that mimics the target model is used; the substitute is differentiable, allowing optimization of ϵ. Adversarial attacks often transfer across models.
ϵ is treated as the adversarial perturbation (parameter) added to the original input x to create the adversarial example x + ϵ.
Adversarial attacks often transfer across models because the crafted perturbations are not tailored to a specific model’s parameters.
Targeted and non‑targeted attacks differ in the attacker’s intent: targeted attacks aim for a specific misclassification, whereas non‑targeted attacks aim only to cause any misclassification.
White‑box and black‑box attacks differ in the attacker’s knowledge of the model: white‑box assumes full knowledge, black‑box assumes none.
Failed defenses include adding noise at test time, averaging many models, weight decay, adding noise at training time, adding adversarial noise at training time, and dropout.
Vanilla RNNs cannot capture long dependencies due to exploding/vanishing gradients.
LSTMs and GRUs are commonly preferred over vanilla RNNs for modeling long dependencies.
RNNs are sequential in nature, making them inefficient for vectorization or GPU parallelization.
Exploding and vanishing gradients hinder learning of long‑range dependencies in vanilla RNNs.
Transformers are introduced to address the sequential inefficiency of RNNs and enable better parallelization.
An attention score indicates the importance of different parts of the data and is used to aggregate the data.
When humans read, they focus on some regions (high resolution) and pay less attention to surrounding areas (low resolution).
Pooling word embeddings using uniform sum or average assumes equal importance of all words, which attention addresses.
Design an attention‑based pooling for classifying tweets by generating a score for each word embedding via a fully‑connected network and normalizing these scores across all words.
c_i = \sum_{j} \alpha_{ij} \, h_j
Words within the tweet are weighted by a softmax over their embeddings, then each embedding is multiplied by its normalized score and summed.
The network is trained end‑to‑end together with the classifier.
Considers interactions between elements from two different sequences (e.g., one sequence in language 1 and another in language 2 for a translator).
Computes attention of the input with respect to itself; for a given token, attention weights are computed for all other tokens in the same sequence.
Methods to compute the attention score between two embeddings a, b \in \mathbb{R}^d.
score(a, b) = a^{\top} b \quad\text{(dot‑product score)}
score(a, b) = \frac{a^{\top} b}{\|a\|\,\|b\|} \quad\text{(cosine similarity)}
score(a, b) = a^{\top} W b \quad\text{(bilinear score)}
score(a, b) = \sigma\bigl(W [a; b]\bigr) \quad\text{(MLP score, where }[a; b]\text{ denotes concatenation)}

A deep‑learning architecture introduced for natural language processing that handles sequential data efficiently via a self‑attention mechanism, capturing long‑range dependencies while being parallelizable and scalable.

Represent the information retrieved or queried based on the relationships between keys and queries; they hold the content or features of each element in the input sequence and are weighted/combined according to attention scores during self‑attention.
RNN with attention versus RNN without attention (illustrated in Figure 7.4).

Queries are used to inquire about the relationships between different elements in the input sequence. They represent a specific element’s information and are compared to keys to determine how relevant each key is to the current query. Queries are typically generated from the input data and used to compute attention scores.

Keys are used to provide context and establish relationships between different elements in the input sequence. They are generated from the input data and are compared to queries to calculate attention scores. Keys help determine how much attention should be given to each value when processing the current query.

In Transformers, attention is modeled as a neural dictionary that retrieves a value v_i for a query q based on a key k_i. Values, queries, and keys are d‑dimensional embeddings. Rather than retrieving a single value, a soft retrieval is performed: all values are retrieved and weighted by the similarity between the query and each key.
attention(q, k, v) = \sum_i \text{similarity}(q, k_i) \times v_i
Let X be an input sequence consisting of n tokens where each token t \in \mathbb{R}^i.

Queries, keys, and values are computed from the same input sequence X using three linear layers: Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V, where X \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times i}, W_Q, W_K \in \mathbb{R}^{i\times k}, W_V \in \mathbb{R}^{i\times v}, Q, K \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times k}, and V \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times v}.
Attention(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{Q K^{T}}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V
Scaled Dot-Product Attention computes attention scores by taking the dot product of queries and keys, scaling by 1/\sqrt{d_k}, applying softmax, and multiplying by the values.
Word2Vec and GloVe models have fixed (static) embeddings for each word, which does not capture contextual meaning.
Attention in transformers allows the context of the sentence to define the meaning and embedding of a word.
To improve performance, the representation space is divided into h sub-spaces, parallel linear layers and attentions are run, and the results are concatenated back to form the original space.
MultiHead(Q, K, V) = Concat(head_1, ..., head_h) W_O where head_i = Attention(Q W_{Q_i}, K W_{K_i}, V W_{V_i})
Each encoder layer consists of a multi-head self-attention sub-layer, a fully-connected sub-layer, and a residual connection around each sub-layer followed by layer normalization.
FFN(x) = max(0, x W_1 + b_1) W_2 + b_2
LayerNorm(x + Sublayer(x))
Since the model lacks recurrent or convolutional layers, positional encoding is added to incorporate order information, allowing the model to attend by relative positions.

RNNs struggle with long-range dependencies, gradient vanishing/explosion, many training steps, and lack parallel computation, whereas Transformers facilitate long-range dependencies, reduce gradient issues, require fewer training steps, and enable parallel computation.
PyTorch implementations of a Transformer Encoder and a Transformer Classifier are provided (Figures 7.11 and 7.12).
A self-supervised objective such as predicting the next token can be used for language modeling.
A residual connection adds the input of a sub-layer to its output before applying layer normalization.
LayerNorm normalizes the summed input across features to stabilize training.
Learn static embeddings; one embedding is used for all senses of a word.
Methods that learn static word embeddings.
Learn contextual embeddings; the embedding of a word changes according to the sentence in which it appears.
Architectures that learn contextual embeddings.
Transformer models that differ in prediction tasks and training objectives, e.g., XLNet, ELMo, GPT‑1/2/3, BERT variants, RoBERTa, Big Bird, etc.

Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers; a Transformer model trained with two self‑supervised tasks (masked word prediction and next sentence prediction); achieves state‑of‑the‑art results on many NLP tasks; exhibits strong transfer‑learning capabilities; used in Google Search to represent queries and documents.
Self‑supervised task where 15 % of tokens are replaced with a [MASK] token; the model predicts the original token using surrounding context; loss is computed only on the masked tokens.

Self‑supervised task that presents two sentences and asks the model to predict whether they appear consecutively in the original text; training data consists of 50 % positive (actual) and 50 % negative (random) sentence pairs.
The practice of pre‑training a model on a large corpus with self‑supervised objectives and then fine‑tuning it on downstream tasks, leveraging the learned representations.
Transformer‑based models for computer‑vision tasks that achieve higher accuracy on large datasets than CNNs due to higher modeling capacity, lower inductive bias, and global receptive fields.
Convolutional Neural Networks; on ImageNet they remain comparable to or better than ViTs in terms of model complexity or size.

Also known as curved or non‑flat spaces, these are mathematical spaces that do not follow the rules of Euclidean geometry. They exhibit different geometric properties compared to Euclidean space (Rⁿ), which is flat and follows Euclid’s axioms.
A mathematical structure consisting of nodes (vertices) and edges (connections) that represent relationships or connections between the nodes.
Property where the learned representation does not change if the input tokens are randomly shuffled; also called permutation‑invariance.
Provides order information to Transformers. Omitting positional encoding causes the model to treat inputs as a set, leading to order‑invariant (permutation‑invariant) behavior.

A modeling approach where the input is treated as a set, requiring permutation‑invariant functions. Useful when the order of items is not essential, such as predicting purchase likelihood of a set of items.
Neural networks designed to operate on graph‑structured (non‑Euclidean) data, extending the capabilities of CNNs and RNNs beyond Euclidean spaces.
CNNs work well on images, RNNs work well on sequences (one‑dimensional); both operate on Euclidean spaces (Rⁿ). GNNs enable modeling predictions for non‑Euclidean spaces such as graphs.
Learn embeddings for each item → Use a shared neural network ψ (e.g., MLP, Transformer, etc.) to project each item to a shared space.
Learn embeddings for the set → Use an order‑invariant aggregation function such as sum, mean, max, etc. to aggregate the embeddings into a single embedding.
Use another neural network ϕ (e.g., MLP) to project the aggregated embedding to the final space.
Graph: G = (V, E, X) is a data‑structure that encodes pair‑wise interactions or relations among concepts and objects.
V is the set of nodes representing concepts or objects.
E ⊆ V × V is the set of edges connecting nodes and representing relations or interactions among them.
X encodes the node features of each node.
Edges can be represented in an adjacency matrix A.
Degree of a node is the number of edges connecting to that node.
Graphs are order‑invariant: the naming or ordering of nodes may be shuffled and the graph will not change.
Functions on graphs must also be order‑invariant.
The transformer learns an N×N attention matrix which represents pairwise importance scores.
Transformer creates a fully‑connected graph over the input and learns the edge weights (attention scores).
Learning edge weights (attention scores) in a fully‑connected graph.
Graph Neural Networks: a class of deep learning models specifically designed to work with structured data represented as graphs.
GNNs are neural networks that function on graphs.

Most graphs are sparse; a dense implementation stores the adjacency matrix in O(N^2) space, whereas sparse representations are more memory‑efficient.
Dense GNNs assume dense connectivity and involve interactions between all nodes.
Sparse GNNs take advantage of the sparsity of the graph and only consider interactions between connected nodes, which is more efficient for sparsely connected graphs.
Batching is done by creating a diagonal matrix of adjacency matrices; large‑scale graph datasets will almost always run out of memory.
Uses an index vector which maps each node to its respective graph in the batch; very simple and efficient.
Sparse implementation is better but dense implementation is more intuitive to implement.
Dense implementation is more intuitive to implement but less memory‑efficient for large graphs.
